comparisons.kpi_name: Does the company have an AI strategy?
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Formal governance structure
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Board-approved Artificial Intelligence Guidelines define TIM’s organizational and governance responsibilities for developing, using, and procuring AI systems across the Group. Oversight is exercised by the Board of Directors (supported by the Control & Risks and Sustainability Committees), with AI-related risks integrated into the Enterprise Risk Management process and monitored via the ERM Steering Committee. Implementation is coordinated through a centralized, cross‑functional team, and the Guidelines are continuously monitored to ensure regulatory compliance for all Group entities and relevant third parties.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation:
- The Board of Directors approved TIM’s Artificial Intelligence Guidelines and ensures alignment with company strategy [2].
- The AI Guidelines define the organizational and governance structure for managing AI issues (development, use, and purchase) [1].
- The Guidelines apply Group-wide to corporate bodies, management, employees, external workers, and certain third parties [3].
- Oversight mechanisms include the Board supported by its committees (Control & Risks, Sustainability) [2].
- AI-related risks are included among key risk factors and are handled within the ERM framework; the ERM Steering Committee meets to monitor risk profile and tolerances [2].
- TIM has adopted centralized governance for AI by establishing a multifunctional, cross‑functional team; the Guidelines are constantly monitored to ensure regulatory compliance and incorporate regulatory changes [1].

comparisons.kpi_name: Percentage of AI initiatives with outcomes tied to measurable business KPIs.
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI use cases with quantitative impact since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Across the TIM Group disclosures from June 2023 to September 2024 only two AI initiatives report hard, numeric results: (1) the Google-Cloud-based voice ­agent rolled out on 24 Sep 2024, credited with a 20 % reduction in average call-handling time and therefore a measurable efficiency gain; (2) TIMFin’s fully-digital lending workflow (AI-driven OCR and credit-scoring) whose 2024 update lists 437 000 device-financing contracts and roughly €33 m of personal-loan disbursements. Sparkle’s AISNA and the Giro d’Italia highlight-selection engine describe benefits qualitatively but publish no numbers, so they are excluded from the quantitative-impact tally, keeping the count at two.

comparisons.kpi_name: Presence of AI talent/experience on the Board
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI talents
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: We identified 84 unique employees currently holding roles at Telecom Italia S.p.A. (TIM) or its direct subsidiaries, whose titles and/or job descriptions align with AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software Implementation, or AI Product Management. Each entry was validated to ensure the individual was currently employed, in scope, and not duplicated, with ambiguous, former, or non-AI-focused roles excluded. This count is based strictly on evidence from organizational sources, company listings, and role definitions [TIM LinkedIn, company website, TheOrg, SAS customer stories, ResearchGate, RocketReach, Gruppo TIM newsroom, SAS.com, TheOrg.com, LinkedIn].

comparisons.kpi_name: Number of use cases  and alignment with strategic priorities
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI use cases with financial impact since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Three of the four identified AI projects explicitly link to economic outcomes: • Google voice agent – 20 % efficiency improvement translates into lower OPEX per handled call; • TIMFin digital lending – 437 k device-financing contracts and ~€33 m personal-loan volume demonstrate direct revenue generation; • Sparkle AISNA – corporate communication emphasises OPEX savings from automated troubleshooting and faster fault resolution. The Giro d’Italia highlight engine is designed for fan engagement and carries no published income or cost figures, so it is excluded. Accordingly, the number of financially impactful AI use cases equals three, which is a logical subset of the four total use cases.

comparisons.kpi_name: When were the first AI solutions executed?
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI related acquisitions since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Among the research set, only one direct AI-related acquisition by Telecom Italia S.p.A. since 2023 is confirmed: the acquisition of TS-Way by TIM's subsidiary Telsy in April 2023, focused on AI-driven cyber threat intelligence [1]. Other events such as joint ventures or investment plans (e.g., Poste Italiane collaborations or Cleafy funding) do not qualify as acquisitions, and thus are excluded. All items were strictly validated for acquisition type, AI relevance, timing, and company linkage.

comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Three of the four identified AI projects explicitly link to economic outcomes: • Google voice agent – 20 % efficiency improvement translates into lower OPEX per handled call; • TIMFin digital lending – 437 k device-financing contracts and ~€33 m personal-loan volume demonstrate direct revenue generation; • Sparkle AISNA – corporate communication emphasises OPEX savings from automated troubleshooting and faster fault resolution. comparisons.kpi_name: Use cases- Number of disclosed AI use cases
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI use cases since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: 4 documented TIM Group AI use cases
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: A review of press releases, event material and segment presentations identifies four distinct AI deployments launched or publicised by the TIM Group from January 2023 onward: 1) Giro d’Italia U23 project (June 2023) – 5G/edge platform with an AI algorithm that automatically selects video highlights for the race app; 2) Sparkle’s AISNA (announced December 2023) – an AI system for autonomous network assurance and traffic-routing optimisation; 3) Google-powered voice agent for TIM’s customer-care lines (live September 2024); 4) TIMFin’s AI-enabled digital lending process (2024 results presentation). No other sources in the dataset describe additional, clearly distinct AI solutions, so the total count stands at four.

comparisons.kpi_name: Number of use cases with financial value
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI use cases with financial impact since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Three of the four identified AI projects explicitly link to economic outcomes: • Google voice agent – 20 % efficiency improvement translates into lower OPEX per handled call; • TIMFin digital lending – 437 k device-financing contracts and ~€33 m personal-loan volume demonstrate direct revenue generation; • Sparkle AISNA – corporate communication emphasises OPEX savings from automated troubleshooting and faster fault resolution. The Giro d’Italia highlight engine is designed for fan engagement and carries no published income or cost figures, so it is excluded. Accordingly, the number of financially impactful AI use cases equals three, which is a logical subset of the four total use cases.

comparisons.kpi_name: Investments in AI-focused companies
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of AI investments since 2023
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: After reviewing all research items, 24 distinct AI-related investments or initiatives by Telecom Italia S.p.A. since January 2023 were identified. Each initiative was validated to ensure it involved direct AI investment or partnership, occurred within the time window, was clearly linked to Telecom Italia S.p.A., and was not a duplicate or ambiguous entry. Overlaps and subsidiaries such as multiple awards from the TIM AI Challenge were counted only once per unique investment, platform, or partnership. Items referring to divestments or lacking post-2023 evidence were excluded to ensure strict alignment with the definition.

comparisons.kpi_name: Partnerships with AI companies
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Name of partnerships
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: Perplexity, Poste Italiane, Expert.ai (Expert System), Google Cloud, Oracle, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services (AWS), NetConsulting Cube, Sparkle, Red Hat, Noovle, BI-REX, BIREX Competence Center, Itential, Intesa Sanpaolo, Broadcom (incl. VMware), TM Forum, National Research Council (CNR), Digitiamo, Covisian, Genesys, Capgemini, Dell Technologies, Juniper Networks, Atos, Salesforce, Nokia, Ericsson, SAP, HP (HPE), Olivetti, SECO, Zerynth, Pedius, Telsy, Forcepoint, RegScale, Afiniti, MASA (Modena Automotive Smart Area), Ducati, 42 Roma Luiss/LUISS University, Scuola Superiore Sant’Anna, MASA, Politecnico di Torino, United Ventures, Opening Future (project with IAG and Google Cloud), GSMA, SYLVA Project, ADIO (Abu Dhabi Investment Office), TM Forum, MASA (Modena Automotive Smart Area), CloudMASA, Telecompaper, Qualcomm Technologies, Fortinet, NetHive.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: To produce this list, I normalized names (merging subsidiaries or joint initiatives—for instance, Sparkle under TIM, Noovle as TIM's cloud company), and combined results across the research set, removing duplicates and entities not meeting the definition of an official AI-related partnership. Each entity listed is confirmed by multiple sources and matches the definition via explicit partnership details, press releases, or inclusion in joint projects. Exclusions included ambiguous mentions with no clear partnership or organizational involvement. For sources, main platforms were referenced such as company websites, Crunchbase, and official newsrooms.

comparisons.kpi_name: Job postings- number of open positions advertised in the public domain
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of publicly advertised in HR/job postings?
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: We identified and strictly deduplicated 55 unique, currently active AI-related roles publicly advertised as HR/job postings by Telecom Italia S.p.A. (including its key subsidiaries Noovle, Telsy, Sparkle, Olivetti, and TIM Brasil). Each role was validated for explicit AI relation (e.g., Data Scientist, Machine Learning Engineer, AI Governance Lead, Generative AI Engineer). We normalized job title variants and removed clear duplicates and ambiguous/unsupported entries, following the provided research data, which includes direct listings on official career portals, recognized job boards, and relevant company subpages [1-65].

comparisons.kpi_name: AI Team Leadership structure- number of leaders (percentage of Job Titles)
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of Board members with AI experience
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Based on the competency matrices and director profiles disclosed in Telecom Italia S.p.A.'s 2024 annual report and investor documentation, there are no individuals currently serving on the Board of Directors or Supervisory Board with proven AI expertise. The most technology-oriented director, Domitilla Benigni, has a background in cybersecurity, not artificial intelligence. Other directors list general digital technology skills but lack evidence of AI-specific experience. This is explicitly stated in the relations data and confirmed by the analysis of board member backgrounds.

comparisons.kpi_name: AI Team Leadership structure- number of leaders (in number)
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: Number of data breaches in past 5 years
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: TIM S.p.A. reports the following "customer data leaks, thefts or losses" (GRI 418-1): 2 incidents in 2023, 1 in 2022, and 0 in 2021 [1]. For 2024, disclosures state that although there was one medium-impact DDoS and two low-impact incidents, there were no data losses or compromise (i.e., zero data-breach cases) [2]. Data for 2020 is not provided in the claims. Summing the reported years within the last five-year window (2021–2024) yields 3 data-breach incidents.

comparisons.kpi_name: AI Ethics role appointed at company, AI risk and management
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: AI Ethics documentation
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: TIM has formal Artificial Intelligence Guidelines, approved by the Board of Directors, that translate ethical principles into operational requirements for the development, use, and procurement of AI systems. The Guidelines define ethical principles, the internal/external regulatory framework (e.g., EU Charter, EU AI Act, GDPR/e‑Privacy, UNESCO AI ethics, EC “Ethics by Design”), and the organizational and governance structure for managing AI. They apply to corporate bodies, management, all employees, external workers and, where required, third‑party partners, are monitored/updated for regulatory changes, and policies are made available via the Group intranet/website.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Claims state TIM has Board‑approved Artificial Intelligence Guidelines that set the regulatory framework and translate ethical principles into day‑to‑day operations, including ethical principles, regulatory references, and governance (approval by Board; framework and content) [1][3]. The Guidelines explicitly reference EU Charter, EU AI Act, GDPR/e‑Privacy, UNESCO AI ethics, and the EC “Ethics by Design” guidance [1]. Scope covers corporate bodies, employees, external workers, and, where required, third parties [7]. Ongoing monitoring and updates for regulatory changes are described [5]. TIM makes policies available on its intranet/website to share contents [1]. Additional communications align with EU “Trustworthy AI” ethics [3].

comparisons.kpi_name: Compliance with AI regulations
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: workplace reduction in FTEs
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: No explicit % or FTE reduction figure reported; only indicative efficiency gains (e.g., 3,700 staff hours/year saved at Sparkle, ~2 FTEs), but no formal headcount reduction attributed directly to AI/automation.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Analysis of public disclosures and internal reports for Telecom Italia S.p.A. reveals that while the company has achieved measurable efficiency gains through AI (e.g., Sparkle's AISNA project saved 3,700 staff hours/year, roughly equivalent to 2 FTEs), there is no formal, company-wide reporting of workforce reduction (either as a % or absolute FTEs) explicitly attributed to automation or AI adoption. Larger workforce reductions at TIM (e.g., from 47,180 to 26,900 employees in 2023-2024) are primarily due to structural changes (NetCo divestiture), not directly to AI. Strategic plans reference AI-driven efficiency, but reductions are achieved via natural attrition and voluntary exits, not explicit AI-linked layoffs. Thus, no concrete % or FTE reduction can be attributed solely to AI/automation based on available evidence.

comparisons.ground_truth_name: AI Ethics documentation
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: TIM has formal Artificial Intelligence Guidelines, approved by the Board of Directors, that translate ethical principles into operational requirements for the development, use, and procurement of AI systems. The Guidelines define ethical principles, the internal/external regulatory framework (e.g., EU Charter, EU AI Act, GDPR/e‑Privacy, UNESCO AI ethics, EC “Ethics by Design”), and the organizational and governance structure for managing AI. They apply to corporate bodies, management, all employees, external workers and, where required, third‑party partners, are monitored/updated for regulatory changes, and policies are made available via the Group intranet/website.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: Claims state TIM has Board‑approved Artificial Intelligence Guidelines that set the regulatory framework and translate ethical principles into day‑to‑day operations, including ethical principles, regulatory references, and governance (approval by Board; framework and content) [1][3]. The Guidelines explicitly reference EU Charter, EU AI Act, GDPR/e‑Privacy, UNESCO AI ethics, and the EC “Ethics by Design” guidance [1]. Scope covers corporate bodies, employees, external workers, and, where required, third parties [7]. Ongoing monitoring and updates for regulatory changes are described [5]. TIM makes policies available on its intranet/website to share contents [1]. Additional communications align with EU “Trustworthy AI” ethics [3].

comparisons.kpi_name: Governance documents published on AI deployment
comparisons.pipeline_rationale: Heuristic fallback scoring based on evidence text.
comparisons.ground_truth_name: First year of AI deployment
comparisons.ground_truth_answer: 2023 — Telecom Italia (TIM) deployed an AI algorithm on its 5G/edge computing platform to automatically select and compile real-time video highlights for the Giro d’Italia U23 cycling race, representing the company’s first publicly documented at-scale AI use in a live production setting.
comparisons.ground_truth_explanation: The Giro d’Italia U23 project is the earliest externally documented instance in which TIM placed an AI system into live, operational use. Announced and executed in 2023, the solution ingested multiple live camera feeds at the network edge and, through machine-learning models, generated highlight clips that were immediately delivered to the race’s mobile application users. No verifiable sources describe an earlier production-scale AI deployment by TIM, while later projects—such as the 2024 Google-powered customer-service voice agent and the 2025 Sparkle AISNA network-assurance platform—occur after 2023. Therefore 2023 is the correct first-deployment year.

unmatched_kpis: C-level statements about AI Strategy
AI communications in social media, white papers (volume and boldness of messaging), limit to G7 or primary markets
Presence and clarity of a publicly communicated AI roadmap outlining long-term objectives, milestones, and investment priorities.
To what extent does the organization allocate and scale financial, human, and technological resources to AI initiatives in a way that delivers measurable business outcomes?
Type of technology, AI model (include agentic AI) point in time implementation or agents Maturity of agentic workforce within the operating model.
Has implementation been primarily an in-house development or partnership model? In-house or outsource some risk, external expertise

To what extent has the company achieved measurable workforce efficiency through AI or GenAI — such as automation, task augmentation, or optimized hiring — resulting in stable or increased output with equal or fewer FTEs?
To what extent has the company operationalized AI — demonstrating tangible rollout and active usage of AI or GenAI in core business operations, workflows, or decision processes at scale?

To what extent has the company maintained data security and confidentiality in its AI operations — including the presence or absence of data breaches, leaks, or other security incidents over the past five years?
To what extent has the company established a mature, enterprise-wide data strategy and foundational architecture that effectively supports AI development and deployment?
To what extent does the company integrate and harmonize data from diverse internal and external sources to make it efficiently usable for AI and analytics applications?
To what extent is the company’s hardware and cloud infrastructure capable of handling expanding AI workloads with high responsiveness, scalability, and cost-efficiency?
To what extent has the company adopted modern, enterprise-grade analytics and AI platforms that streamline model development, deployment, monitoring, and lifecycle management?
To what extent are the company’s processes and tools for developing, deploying, monitoring, and improving ML models mature, automated, and aligned with production standards?
To what extent has the company implemented robust mechanisms that safeguard data against unauthorized access or misuse, ensuring compliance with privacy and security regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA)?
To what extent is the company modernizing or integrating legacy systems into its current data and AI architecture to eliminate bottlenecks and data silos?
To what extent does the company explore and adopt complementary frontier technologies (e.g., IoT, blockchain, edge computing, AR/VR, quantum computing) that enhance or expand AI capabilities?
Training- disclosed training efforts and breadth of the activity (public disclosure)

Job titles- number of employees in AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software implementation, AI product management (other roles to be discussed) - Experience description demonstrates AI skill base or focus (percentage of total employees)

Job titles- number of employees in AI Development, Data Engineering, AI Software implementation, AI product management (other roles to be discussed) - Experience description demonstrates AI skill base or focus (in number)
What department “owns” AI strategy? Do they have a tech department that has its own Board member?
To what extent does the company actively support employee well-being and engagement throughout its AI transformation journey?
How effectively does the organization foster international and multicultural collaboration in AI development and deployment?
How strongly is an AI-driven and innovation-oriented mindset embedded in the company’s organizational culture?
How mature and effective are the company’s change management and communication practices in enabling AI adoption?
How well are cross-functional collaboration and governance structures established to coordinate AI initiatives across departments?
To what degree has the company built AI literacy and encouraged knowledge sharing across the workforce?
How ready is the company’s culture to embrace data-driven decision-making and continuous AI-enabled improvement?
How does the company integrate community engagement and social responsibility into its AI initiatives?
To what extent does the company promote an ethical culture and mechanisms for continuous improvement in AI practices?
Open source- overall engagement with open source development ecosystem, code repositories, open source projects (i.e. GitHub)
How explicitly does AI enhance the customer value proposition (e.g., personalization, NPS, retention) and tie to growth/margin outcomes?
Evidence that AI strategy targets disruptive differentiation (new AI‑native offerings, pricing models, or channels) beyond efficiency.
How clear are cross‑functional roles and responsibilities for AI (business, product, data, risk, legal) from idea to run?
How systematically are external benchmarks used to drive AI delivery performance (speed, quality, cost) and improvement targets?
What business‑visible monitoring exists for models in production (performance, drift, data quality, incidents) and who sees it?
Is there a standardized, auditable methodology to estimate and realize ROI for AI (ex‑ante and ex‑post)?
How well is delivery aligned with applicable AI/sector regulations during rollout and operation (e.g., DPIA, model cards, DSARs)?
How mature and reproducible is the model development workflow (from experimentation to CI/CD and release)?
Do users have a governed self‑service data & AI portal/marketplace (catalog, access control, usage analytics)?
Is there an enterprise‑wide canonical data model that underpins AI/analytics across domains?
How complete and enforced are data & model and policies/standards (quality, lineage, privacy, retention, access)?
To what extent is metadata (technical + business) captured and used to improve discovery, lineage, and MDM?
Is risk appetite for AI explicitly defined (by domain/use case) and used for approvals and guardrails?
How visible and concrete is the company’s public advocacy and action on responsible AI (AI‑for‑Good)?
Are role‑based learning paths and curricula established for AI (from execs to engineers) with certification?
How structured and scalable is experimentation (PoCs/pilots) with clear success metrics and governance?
Are there structured alliances/JVs to accelerate AI value with shared IP, data, or distribution?
Do standardized, privacy‑preserving data‑sharing mechanisms exist across the ecosystem?
Is the firm actively shaping AI standards and regulation via consortia, position papers, and pilots?
How rigorous is partner/vendor capability assessment (security, compliance, quality, delivery, integration)?
unmatched_data_points: AI Talent broken down into type of AI Talent (1. AI & ML Engineering 2. Data & Analytics, 3. NLP & Generative AI, 4. AI Product & Strategy)
2 year revenue growth CAGR for latest fiscal year
AI ethics governance model yes or no
